item.title: This Keychron V3 mechanical keyboard is must-buy at 50% off
item.itunes:title: This Keychron V3 mechanical keyboard is must-buy at 50% off
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Take 50% off for a limited time
Keychron V3
$45 $90 Save $45

Keychron makes some of the best mechanical keyboards you can buy right now. So, when one of its products gets a deep discount, it's a good idea to try and grab one while the deal's still live. This Keychron V3 TKL is a fantastic model that delivers whether you plan to use it for gaming or in the office. Right now, you can score 50% off this model for a limited time, knocking it down to just $45.

$45 at Amazon

It might be hard to believe, but changing to a new keyboard can make a world of difference. Now, it's going to really depend on what type of keyboard you're upgrading from, but if you've never used or owned a mechanical keyboard — we think you're missing out. Not only are you going to get a better typing experience, but it's also going to be a product that lasts and at the same time offers some repairability as well.

Related
Best mechanical keyboards in 2024

Check out some of the best mechanical keyboards that you should be buying this year, ranging from full-size to compact 60%.

With that said, we think this model from Keychron is going to be a stellar choice, mainly because it offers so much for so little money. While the V3 model typically comes priced at $90, it can now be had for far less from Amazon, with a discount that's currently knocking 50% off. That brings the price down to just $44.99 for a limited time. But be quick, supplies won't last long with this incredible deal.

What's great about Keychron's V3 mechanical keyboard?

Source: Keychron

There are a lot of things we love about this keyboard. Not only are you going to get awesome durability thanks to its multi-layer construction, but you're also going to get a rather compact keyboard thanks to its TKL 80% layout. Of course, this keyboard is a lot about providing a comfortable typing experience, and you'll really feel that with its PBT key caps, hot swappable switches, and stabilizers.

The keyboard also offers little perks here and there as well, with south-facing RGB lighting, a 1,000Hz polling rate that delivers a more accurate typing experience, and customization via support for QMK/VIA. Perhaps best of all is that you can this keyboard with either Windows, Linux, or macOS. There's also a built-in customizable knob that can be used to enhance your everyday workflow.

Overall, you are getting a fantastic keyboard with this unit, and it comes in at a price that's hard to pass up. It's versatile, durable, and customizable, which makes it perfect for gaming and even the office. You really can't go wrong with this model, so if you've been looking to get a new keyboard, give this one a try. Of course, if you're still on the fence, we also have some other great keyboard recommendations that you can check out.

item.title: Best PSUs for AMD Ryzen 9950X in 2024
item.itunes:title: Best PSUs for AMD Ryzen 9950X in 2024
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
When building your new PC, the power supply unit (PSU) will provide power to all connected components. Be it your motherboard, CPU, RAM, SSDs, GPU, and case fans. They all draw power through the PSU. It's vital to spend a good amount of money on the PSU and purchase one from a reputable brand to ensure you have adequate protection and it's reliable for daily use. This is the only barrier between the national grid and your expensive PC parts.

The AMD Ryzen 9 9950X is a seriously powerful processor and can suck up to 200W of power through the motherboard. Install a powerful graphics card and you'll easily surpass 600W with everything factored in. So which PSU should you buy? Is a 750W unit enough or is it better to buy a PSU that can handle more than 1,000W of power? Whether you're building a standard ATX PC or a compact small form factor (SFF) system, we've got a PSU for you.

  • Editor's choice
    Corsair RM750E

    The Corsair RM750e is a decent mid-range PSU. It features 750W of total power output, so it's powerful enough to handle everything below the most demanding hardware configurations. It also features 80+ gold power efficiency and fully modular cables and is one of our favorite choices for a new build.

    $100 at Best Buy $100 at Amazon $100 at Corsair
  • Best budget
    Thermaltake Toughpower GX2 600W

    If you're looking for an affordable power supply, check out the Thermaltake Toughpower GX2. This PSU offers a total power output of 600W, and while it's not modular, it is more than capable of powering a mid-tier gaming build with the Ryzen 9 9950X at the center.

    $70 at Best Buy $70 at Amazon
  • Best-looking PSU
    ASUS ROG Loki SFX-L 850W
    $198 $240 Save $42

    The Asus ROG Loki SFX-L 850W is a solid power supply with a capacity of 850W. There's RGB lighting on the fan, which doesn't matter much, but it's a good-looking PSU regardless.

    $240 at Newegg $198 at Amazon
  • Best quiet PSU
    be quiet! BN515 Straight Power 12-850W
    $160 $210 Save $50

    If you hate a noisy PC, check out be quiet!'s BN515 Straight Power 12-850W. This PSU offers a total power output of 850W, so it's capable of handling even the heaviest of workloads. It also runs nearly silently, thanks to be quiet!'s brand of fan installed in the PSU.

    $160 at Newegg $210 at Amazon
  • Best capacity PSU
    EVGA Supernova 1000 G7

    The EVGA Supernova 1000 G7 is a high-quality power supply unit that can last you a long time. It has a high-efficiency rating of 80 Plus Gold and provides built-in LEDs to allow real-time load monitoring.

    $180 at Amazon
  • Best PSU for cable management
    SeaSonic Connect Comprise Prime 750W

    Cable management can be the most difficult part of building a PC, and if you struggle with it, check out the SeaSonic Connect Comprise Prime 750W. The SeaSonic Connect features a unique design that not only makes cable management easier, but also the reduced cable size helps improve airflow in your case.

    $190 at Newegg
  • All-white PSU
    ASUS ROG Strix 850W White Edition PSU

    The ASUS ROG Strix 850W White Edition is the PSU to buy if you're going for an all-white PC build. This is one of the most powerful power supplies that comes with a white chassis and has a clean aesthetic. It does lack RGB, making it a great option for anyone who doesn't like lights in their PSU.

    $190 at Newegg $169 at Amazon
  • Best value
    Thermaltake Toughpower Grand RGB 650W PSU

    The Thermaltake Toughpower Grand RGB 650W is an ideal PSU for anyone building with the AMD Ryzen 9 9950X processor who wants an affordable RGB setup. It offers 650W, which is enough for gamers and anyone not using the most intensive hardware.

    $152 at Newegg $113 at Amazon

Choosing a power supply for the AMD Ryzen 9 9950X

The AMD Ryzen 9 9950X is a power-efficient processor, considering it has 16 cores and 32 threads. You won't need the highest-capacity PSU on the market to provide enough juice, but how much capacity you will need depends on other factors, including the graphics card. The GPU can easily draw the most current, so it's best to configure your PC accordingly, taking into account the maximum draw rating from AMD, Intel, or Nvidia (depending on the manufacturer).

It's recommended to calculate your PC power requirements and then purchase a PSU with enough capacity to run at around 50% for maximum efficiency. This is where the power supply works at its best, converting AC to DC and losing as little power as possible.

Related
Best PSU for gaming in 2024

If you're looking for the best power supply units on the market to power your gaming time, we've got you covered with this collection.

item.title: 7 things you need to look for when buying a NAS
item.itunes:title: 7 things you need to look for when buying a NAS
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • More data is being generated at home and in offices. Network-attached storage (NAS) is key to store everything in a single location.
  • Choosing a NAS involves considering drive bays, CPU, RAM, networking ports, operating system, power requirements, and size.
  • NAS enclosures become more expensive with better features. Price depends on intended use, so consider your needs and budget.

More data than ever is being generated at home and in offices and network-attached storage (NAS) has proven a reliable method of storing everything in a single location. They're essentially high-capacity storage servers without all the technical overhead. If you're unfamiliar with server management, a NAS enclosure can be an entry point to store lots of data without getting your hands dirty. Just about any device can connect and transfer data to the NAS, including smartphones, tablets, laptops, desktop PCs, and other storage services. But how do you choose a NAS? Here are some handy pointers to bear in mind when shopping around.

Related
Best NAS devices in 2024

Expand your PC storage with one of these NAS enclosures

1 Drive bays and capacity

One of the most vital specifications of any NAS is the number of drive bays. This will determine how much data can be stored on the device. One 3.5-inch drive bay can take a standard mechanical drive, which can be bought with a capacity of up to 24TB per drive. A NAS with five drive bays could take five HDDs with a maximum raw capacity of 120TB before factoring in a redundant array of independent/inexpensive disks (RAID). The more drive bays your NAS has, the more you can store on it.

Not all bays need to be populated either. You can buy a 10-bay enclosure with the plan to fill it with drives over time but start with just two HDDs installed. Even with a RAID configured, new drives can be added without breaking everything. Like a GPU where it's recommended to spend as much as possible to avoid upgrading early, the same goes with a NAS and its drive bays. I recommend buying one that has more bays than you need to leave room for expansion. You don't want to run out of space.

Related
What is RAID (and how to set it up in a NAS)

RAID is worth checking out if you desire fast transfer speeds or want to protect your data against drive corruption (or even both, at the same time)

4

2 CPU and RAM

The processor largely dictates what can be done on the NAS. This is followed up closely by system memory (or RAM). The more powerful the chip, the more apps and services can be run on the server. If all you're doing on the NAS is storing some files and uploading backups from various devices, a low-power ARM processor will do just fine. Intel and AMD chips are only required if you plan on running services and storing data.

Hardware transcoding and virtualization can be resource-intensive and require enough RAM and computing power to avoid system slowdown. Most NAS enclosures from brands including TerraMaster, Synology, and Asustor will include at least 1GB of RAM. This is sufficient for running the OS and allowing a few devices to connect and move data to and from the storage pool. 2GB or more is required for everything else. It's also worth checking to see if the NAS allows for the RAM to be expanded or upgraded.

3 Price

If price is important to your decision-making, NAS enclosures will generally become more expensive the better the features and specifications. A NAS with a single 1GbE networking link, two drive bays, and an ARM processor will be considerably cheaper than an Intel-powered NAS with 4GB of upgradable RAM, four drive bays, two 2.5GbE network ports, and a few M.2 SSD slots. Again, the price of the NAS depends on what you plan to use the enclosure for. Simple file storage will allow you to save the most.

NAS enclosures don't typically come with drives installed, so the enclosure price excludes how much additional cost will be required for storing data.

Related
How to secure your NAS and keep your data safe

Keep your data safe from natural disasters and prying eyes.

2

4 Networking ports

A single 1GbE network link will be sufficient for transfer rates of up to 100MB/s. A typical hard disk drive (HDD) will support speeds up to around 500MB/s and a solid-state drive (SSD) can hit 15,000MB/s, so long as the system has enough resources to facilitate such transfers. If you'll move larger files across the network, a NAS with dual 1GbE or 2.5GbE ports may be better suited to your needs. 10GbE SFP+ ports are ideal for those with more advanced networking infrastructure.

5 Operating system

The operating system is the heart and soul of a NAS, allowing you to store files, run apps and services, and much more. If you're looking at a NAS from Synology or some other brand, it will likely come with a proprietary OS. This is great if you want to enjoy first-party support and guaranteed performance with the enclosure. But you will risk hitting the end of life when the company offers limited security updates. An OS developed outside the company, such as Unraid or TrueNAS doesn't have this issue.

An OS such as Unraid should work on most enclosures that support the sideloading of the OS, though your mileage may vary, especially if it's one of the more affordable models. Some prebuilt NAS enclosures, including the LincPlus LincStation N1, come with aftermarket operating systems but most of them will work similarly. We've rounded up some of the best operating systems to use on a NAS, so check out that guide for some ideas.

Related
Building your own NAS? These 5 operating systems are the best

If you prefer building your own NAS from scratch, here are five operating systems you need to check out

2

6 Power requirements

A NAS typically draws little power when compared to a desktop system, but the NAS will (hopefully) remain online, which can eat into your electricity budget. So long as you're using less than 50 watts, you're in a good position. The Synology DiskStation DS923+ is a powerful AMD NAS but it's only rated to draw 35 watts under full load. Unless you'll hammer your storage server hard all day long, you shouldn't notice much of an impact on your annual utility bill.

7 Size and footprint

The less powerful a NAS, the smaller it will be. This is due to the lower number of drive bays, the fewer fans to keep everything running cool, and the smaller PCB. A dual-bay Synology DiskStation DS223j can be installed almost anywhere in the home, so long as there's easy access to the LAN. The Synology DiskStation DS3622xs+ will require careful consideration with its 12 drive bays and 10kg weight. Rackmount NAS servers can be a good way to save space, but they require a server cabinet.

A NAS with five drive bays could take five HDDs with a maximum raw capacity of 120TB before factoring in a redundant array of independent/inexpensive disks (RAID). The more drive bays your NAS has, the more you can store on it.

Not all bays need to be populated either. You can buy a 10-bay enclosure with the plan to fill it with drives over time but start with just two HDDs installed. Even with a RAID configured, new drives can be added without breaking everything. Like a GPU where it's recommended to spend as much as possible to avoid upgrading early, the same goes with a NAS and its drive bays. I recommend buying one that has more bays than you need to leave room for expansion. You don't want to run out of space.

Related
What is RAID (and how to set it up in a NAS)

RAID is worth checking out if you desire fast transfer speeds or want to protect your data against drive corruption (or even both, at the same time)

4

2 CPU and RAM

The processor largely dictates what can be done on the NAS. This is followed up closely by system memory (or RAM). The more powerful the chip, the more apps and services can be run on the server. If all you're doing on the NAS is storing some files and uploading backups from various devices, a low-power ARM processor will do just fine. Intel and AMD chips are only required if you plan on running services and storing data.

Hardware transcoding and virtualization can be resource-intensive and require enough RAM and computing power to avoid system slowdown. Most NAS enclosures from brands including TerraMaster, Synology, and Asustor will include at least 1GB of RAM. This is sufficient for running the OS and allowing a few devices to connect and move data to and from the storage pool. 2GB or more is required for everything else. It's also worth checking to see if the NAS allows for the RAM to be expanded or upgraded.

3 Price

If price is important to your decision-making, NAS enclosures will generally become more expensive the better the features and specifications. A NAS with a single 1GbE networking link, two drive bays, and an ARM processor will be considerably cheaper than an Intel-powered NAS with 4GB of upgradable RAM, four drive bays, two 2.5GbE network ports, and a few M.2 SSD slots. Again, the price of the NAS depends on what you plan to use the enclosure for. Simple file storage will allow you to save the most.

NAS enclosures don't typically come with drives installed, so the enclosure price excludes how much additional cost will be required for storing data.

Related
How to secure your NAS and keep your data safe

Keep your data safe from natural disasters and prying eyes.

2

4 Networking ports

A single 1GbE network link will be sufficient for transfer rates of up to 100MB/s. A typical hard disk drive (HDD) will support speeds up to around 500MB/s and a solid-state drive (SSD) can hit 15,000MB/s, so long as the system has enough resources to facilitate such transfers. If you'll move larger files across the network, a NAS with dual 1GbE or 2.5GbE ports may be better suited to your needs. 10GbE SFP+ ports are ideal for those with more advanced networking infrastructure.

5 Operating system

The operating system is the heart and soul of a NAS, allowing you to store files, run apps and services, and much more. If you're looking at a NAS from Synology or some other brand, it will likely come with a proprietary OS. This is great if you want to enjoy first-party support and guaranteed performance with the enclosure. But you will risk hitting the end of life when the company offers limited security updates. An OS developed outside the company, such as Unraid or TrueNAS doesn't have this issue.

An OS such as Unraid should work on most enclosures that support the sideloading of the OS, though your mileage may vary, especially if it's one of the more affordable models. Some prebuilt NAS enclosures, including the LincPlus LincStation N1, come with aftermarket operating systems but most of them will work similarly. We've rounded up some of the best operating systems to use on a NAS, so check out that guide for some ideas.

Related
Building your own NAS? These 5 operating systems are the best

If you prefer building your own NAS from scratch, here are five operating systems you need to check out

2

6 Power requirements

A NAS typically draws little power when compared to a desktop system, but the NAS will (hopefully) remain online, which can eat into your electricity budget. So long as you're using less than 50 watts, you're in a good position. The Synology DiskStation DS923+ is a powerful AMD NAS but it's only rated to draw 35 watts under full load. Unless you'll hammer your storage server hard all day long, you shouldn't notice much of an impact on your annual utility bill.

7 Size and footprint

The less powerful a NAS, the smaller it will be. This is due to the lower number of drive bays, the fewer fans to keep everything running cool, and the smaller PCB. A dual-bay Synology DiskStation DS223j can be installed almost anywhere in the home, so long as there's easy access to the LAN. The Synology DiskStation DS3622xs+ will require careful consideration with its 12 drive bays and 10kg weight. Rackmount NAS servers can be a good way to save space, but they require a server cabinet.
item.content:encoded:
Key Takeaways

  • More data is being generated at home and in offices. Network-attached storage (NAS) is key to store everything in a single location.
  • Choosing a NAS involves considering drive bays, CPU, RAM, networking ports, operating system, power requirements, and size.
  • NAS enclosures become more expensive with better features. Price depends on intended use, so consider your needs and budget.

More data than ever is being generated at home and in offices and network-attached storage (NAS) has proven a reliable method of storing everything in a single location. They're essentially high-capacity storage servers without all the technical overhead. If you're unfamiliar with server management, a NAS enclosure can be an entry point to store lots of data without getting your hands dirty. Just about any device can connect and transfer data to the NAS, including smartphones, tablets, laptops, desktop PCs, and other storage services. But how do you choose a NAS? Here are some handy pointers to bear in mind when shopping around.

Related
Best NAS devices in 2024

Expand your PC storage with one of these NAS enclosures

1 Drive bays and capacity

One of the most vital specifications of any NAS is the number of drive bays. This will determine how much data can be stored on the device. One 3.5-inch drive bay can take a standard mechanical drive, which can be bought with a capacity of up to 24TB per drive. A NAS with five drive bays could take five HDDs with a maximum raw capacity of 120TB before factoring in a redundant array of independent/inexpensive disks (RAID). The more drive bays your NAS has, the more you can store on it.

Not all bays need to be populated either. You can buy a 10-bay enclosure with the plan to fill it with drives over time but start with just two HDDs installed. Even with a RAID configured, new drives can be added without breaking everything. Like a GPU where it's recommended to spend as much as possible to avoid upgrading early, the same goes with a NAS and its drive bays. I recommend buying one that has more bays than you need to leave room for expansion. You don't want to run out of space.

Related
What is RAID (and how to set it up in a NAS)

RAID is worth checking out if you desire fast transfer speeds or want to protect your data against drive corruption (or even both, at the same time)

4

2 CPU and RAM

The processor largely dictates what can be done on the NAS. This is followed up closely by system memory (or RAM). The more powerful the chip, the more apps and services can be run on the server. If all you're doing on the NAS is storing some files and uploading backups from various devices, a low-power ARM processor will do just fine. Intel and AMD chips are only required if you plan on running services and storing data.

Hardware transcoding and virtualization can be resource-intensive and require enough RAM and computing power to avoid system slowdown. Most NAS enclosures from brands including TerraMaster, Synology, and Asustor will include at least 1GB of RAM. This is sufficient for running the OS and allowing a few devices to connect and move data to and from the storage pool. 2GB or more is required for everything else. It's also worth checking to see if the NAS allows for the RAM to be expanded or upgraded.

3 Price

If price is important to your decision-making, NAS enclosures will generally become more expensive the better the features and specifications. A NAS with a single 1GbE networking link, two drive bays, and an ARM processor will be considerably cheaper than an Intel-powered NAS with 4GB of upgradable RAM, four drive bays, two 2.5GbE network ports, and a few M.2 SSD slots. Again, the price of the NAS depends on what you plan to use the enclosure for. Simple file storage will allow you to save the most.

NAS enclosures don't typically come with drives installed, so the enclosure price excludes how much additional cost will be required for storing data.

Related
How to secure your NAS and keep your data safe

Keep your data safe from natural disasters and prying eyes.

2

4 Networking ports

A single 1GbE network link will be sufficient for transfer rates of up to 100MB/s. A typical hard disk drive (HDD) will support speeds up to around 500MB/s and a solid-state drive (SSD) can hit 15,000MB/s, so long as the system has enough resources to facilitate such transfers. If you'll move larger files across the network, a NAS with dual 1GbE or 2.5GbE ports may be better suited to your needs. 10GbE SFP+ ports are ideal for those with more advanced networking infrastructure.

5 Operating system

The operating system is the heart and soul of a NAS, allowing you to store files, run apps and services, and much more. If you're looking at a NAS from Synology or some other brand, it will likely come with a proprietary OS. This is great if you want to enjoy first-party support and guaranteed performance with the enclosure. But you will risk hitting the end of life when the company offers limited security updates. An OS developed outside the company, such as Unraid or TrueNAS doesn't have this issue.

An OS such as Unraid should work on most enclosures that support the sideloading of the OS, though your mileage may vary, especially if it's one of the more affordable models. Some prebuilt NAS enclosures, including the LincPlus LincStation N1, come with aftermarket operating systems but most of them will work similarly. We've rounded up some of the best operating systems to use on a NAS, so check out that guide for some ideas.

Related
Building your own NAS? These 5 operating systems are the best

If you prefer building your own NAS from scratch, here are five operating systems you need to check out

2

6 Power requirements

A NAS typically draws little power when compared to a desktop system, but the NAS will (hopefully) remain online, which can eat into your electricity budget. So long as you're using less than 50 watts, you're in a good position. The Synology DiskStation DS923+ is a powerful AMD NAS but it's only rated to draw 35 watts under full load. Unless you'll hammer your storage server hard all day long, you shouldn't notice much of an impact on your annual utility bill.

7 Size and footprint

The less powerful a NAS, the smaller it will be. This is due to the lower number of drive bays, the fewer fans to keep everything running cool, and the smaller PCB. A dual-bay Synology DiskStation DS223j can be installed almost anywhere in the home, so long as there's easy access to the LAN. The Synology DiskStation DS3622xs+ will require careful consideration with its 12 drive bays and 10kg weight. Rackmount NAS servers can be a good way to save space, but they require a server cabinet.

item.title: Here's how I take stunning pictures of the night sky using the Astrophotography mode on Google Pixel phones
item.itunes:title: Here's how I take stunning pictures of the night sky using the Astrophotography mode on Google Pixel phones
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Google's Pixel phones are considered second-to-none in the camera department, as they take advantage of the company's amazing software to produce stunning shots using unique features. The Pixel phones particularly excel in low-light photography, thanks to the Night Sight feature that debuted in 2018.

But what really impressed me was the Astrophotography mode that arrived a year later, and it remains one of the most popular Pixel features that uses Google's algorithmic magic to deliver stunning results. Now, a recent update to the Pixel Camera app lets you manually choose "Astro mode" whenever you'd like. If this is your first time using a Pixel phone, and you're wondering how to use the astrophotography mode, then you've come to the right place.

Related
Best smartphone cameras in 2024

We've tried them all, so here are our picks

What is the astrophotography mode?

How does it even work?

Google's astrophotography mode is built into the stock camera app that ships with the Pixel phones. It's been a part of the stock camera app since 2019 when it arrived with the Pixel 4, and it's still here on the new Pixel 9 series, too. The astrophotography mode is available on Pixel 3 and newer Pixel phones, so all modern Google phones can use the company's computational photography features to turn your boring night sky into a magical scenery.

Previously, enabling the astrophotography mode on the Pixel Camera app wasn't as straightforward. It used to appear only when the conditions were right; however, an update to version 9.5.118 or newer lets users activate Astro mode manually.

If you don't see a manual toggle for Astro mode in the Night Sight tab, make sure your Pixel Camera app is updated to the latest version.

In this guide, we'll show you how to enable this photography mode automatically by setting the right scene and how to do it manually. I've highlighted how I enable it on my Google Pixel 7 and Pixel 9 below, but you can follow the same steps on all Pixel phones that support the feature.

How to enable and use astrophotography mode on Pixel phones

The old way, which automatically activates when the conditions are right

  1. Launch the camera app and go to the Night Sight mode by swiping through various modes at the bottom of the app.
  2. Select the Settings button located in the top left corner of the screen, and open the Night Sight camera settings.
  3. On the settings overlay, ensure that the Astrophotography option is set to Auto.

The next thing you need to do is set your phone on a steady surface like a tripod or a rock. Once you do that, the shutter button will change from a moon icon to stars. This is a crucial step because the Astrophotography mode won't kick in unless you keep your device steady. Even the smallest of movements will trigger an alert asking you to keep your phone steady.

I recommend using a tripod to ensure your Pixel phone is ready to capture astrophotography photos. I'm leaving a link to one of my favorite tripods below, but feel free to use any tripod for this job.

Joby GripTight ONE GorillaPod Stand
$20 $35 Save $15

The Joby GripTight ONE GorillaPod Stand is the perfect accessory to pair with your smartphone if you capture a lot of photos or record videos. You can use this mount with any smartphone as long as it fits the specifications.

$20 at Amazon

Google's computational photography is quite powerful, but you won't walk away with good results unless you're shooting in favorable conditions for astrophotography. The first thing you need to do is step away from the bustling city lights to keep the light pollution in check. It's also important to ensure that there are no major light sources for at least 10 miles from your shooting spot. Having a lot of light in your shot will result in extremely bright photos due to the long exposure times, so I recommend using apps like Light Pollution Map to find dark areas around you.

Similarly, it's important to ensure there are no clouds between you and the stars. A cloudy sky will block all the stars and make the entire process more difficult. You can check out the best weather apps on the Google Play Store to find the right time for this starry adventure.

Unlike the Astrophoto mode in Samsung's Expert RAW camera app, Google's stock camera app doesn't show you what you're looking at in the night sky. You'll definitely need a sky guide to identify stars and constellations. Luckily, there are apps on the Google Play Store that'll do the job for you; I recommend 'Stellarium Mobile' for this because it's easy to use and gives you all the data you need in real time.

With a tripod and a bit of control over some external factors, you are now ready to start capturing the night sky. Here's how to do it:

  1. Place your phone on the tripod, pointing the cameras towards the sky.
  2. Check the viewfinder to adjust the frame to your liking.
  3. As soon as you set up the frame and the phone stands steadily in place, you'll notice that the moon icon on the shutter button will change to stars. This indicates that the astrophotography mode is turned on.
  4. Once you are ready to shoot, simply press the shutter button and let the Pixel work its magic.

The manual way to use Astro mode on your Pixel

Google updated the Pixel Camera app in August 2024 to add a manual toggle for Astro mode under the Night Sight tab. While this is certainly more convenient, you may still want to use the aforementioned methods, because they'll ensure the conditions are right for astrophotography. If you tick the manual toggle for Astro mode without the scene being right, you might not notice the difference between Astro mode, Night Sight, and standard shooting modes. With that out of the way, here's how to manually activate Astro mode on your Pixel.

I used Astro mode on my Google Pixel 9, but it'll be available on devices that can run Pixel Camera app version 9.5.118 or higher. The screenshots taken below were during the day and are for reference only — you'll want to use Astro mode at night when the stars and moon are out.

  1. Open the Pixel Camera app.
  2. Swipe right in the list of shooting modes to find Night Sight.
  3. Press the button in the lower right corner of the screen, which controls your shutter speed.
  4. A new slider will appear on the lower portion of your viewfinder controlling the shutter speed. Swipe all the way right until you hit Astro mode.
  5. Tap the shutter button to start a five-second timer. This will give you time to mount your phone to a tripod or place it on a secure surface before Astro mode starts working.
  6. That's it! You've now manually activated Astro mode and can take beautiful photos of the sky with long shutter speeds to get better images.

Tips & tricks for getting the best results

Whichever way you use astrophotography mode on your Pixel, these tips will help you get the best result. It's best to leave the phone alone while it's capturing and processing the image. You'll see a countdown timer on the display that tells you how long it needs to capture the image. The phone will also alert you with a chime when it's done shooting and processing.

From here, you are ready to share the image or run it through an editing app to process it further. A bit of editing will go a long way in ensuring your astrophotography photos look perfect, but it's entirely optional.

Google's Pixel phones are great for night photography

And that's how you can use the astrophotography mode on Pixel phones. The process remains largely the same for pretty much all Pixel phones that support astrophotography, but the results may vary. Astrophotography is just one of the many great features you'll find on Google's new Pixel phones. The Pixel 9 is one of the latest Pixel phones to have it, but many great devices can also take advantage of Astro mode, like the Pixel 8 Pro.

Related
Google Pixel 8 review: The best base model flagship of them all

The Pixel 8 brings a new chip with some very capable AI prowess, but the rest of the package is also polished and near Pro level

  1. Open the Pixel Camera app.
  2. Swipe right in the list of shooting modes to find Night Sight.
  3. Press the button in the lower right corner of the screen, which controls your shutter speed.
  4. A new slider will appear on the lower portion of your viewfinder controlling the shutter speed. Swipe all the way right until you hit Astro mode.
  5. Tap the shutter button to start a five-second timer. This will give you time to mount your phone to a tripod or place it on a secure surface before Astro mode starts working.
  6. That's it! You've now manually activated Astro mode and can take beautiful photos of the sky with long shutter speeds to get better images.

Tips & tricks for getting the best results

Whichever way you use astrophotography mode on your Pixel, these tips will help you get the best result. It's best to leave the phone alone while it's capturing and processing the image. You'll see a countdown timer on the display that tells you how long it needs to capture the image. The phone will also alert you with a chime when it's done shooting and processing.

From here, you are ready to share the image or run it through an editing app to process it further. A bit of editing will go a long way in ensuring your astrophotography photos look perfect, but it's entirely optional.

Google's Pixel phones are great for night photography

And that's how you can use the astrophotography mode on Pixel phones. The process remains largely the same for pretty much all Pixel phones that support astrophotography, but the results may vary. Astrophotography is just one of the many great features you'll find on Google's new Pixel phones. The Pixel 9 is one of the latest Pixel phones to have it, but many great devices can also take advantage of Astro mode, like the Pixel 8 Pro.

Related
Google Pixel 8 review: The best base model flagship of them all

The Pixel 8 brings a new chip with some very capable AI prowess, but the rest of the package is also polished and near Pro level

item.title: Intel releases a swath of Xeon news, including a 17x improvement in AI processing
item.itunes:title: Intel releases a swath of Xeon news, including a 17x improvement in AI processing
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Intel aims for 17x better AI performance with next-gen Xeon 6 chips.
  • Xeon W-3500 and W-2500 workstation CPUs offer enhanced AI capabilities.
  • Workstation processors boast higher frequencies, memory support, and AI training speeds.

With Intel in the middle of an AI-based hardware race, it's pulling out as many stops as it can. We've seen it discuss how it wants to achieve 100 TOPS of AI performance in its next generation of CPUs, but it also wants to discuss what it's up to with its new Xeon processors. Turns out, Intel has been hard at work ensuring that its Xeon 6 chips can handle AI workloads, improving its performance by 17x over four years.

Related
Intel 15th-gen Arrow Lake: Everything we're expecting from Team Blue

Here's everything we know about Intel's next-gen processors.

Intel states that Xeon 6 will feature 17x better AI performance

As announced in an Intel press release, the company has been improving how well its new Xeon processors can tackle AI tasks. Roger Chandler, the Intel vice president and general manager, had a lot of good things to say about the company's advancements:

“The newest MLPerf results show how continued investment and resourcing is critical for improving AI performance. Over the past four years, we have raised the bar for AI performance on Intel Xeon processors by up to 17x based on MLPerf. As we near general availability later this year, we look forward to ramping Xeon 6 with our customers and partners.”

Intel breaks down more what it means with its 17x claim in the press release. The figure is based on the AI processing power of the third generation of Xeon CPUs when computing a natural language processing model. Even when Intel compares Xeon 6 with the fifth-generation chips, it still manages an impressive increase of 1.9x in AI processing.

Intel also announces the Xeon W-3500 and W-2500 workstation CPUs

Image Credit: Intel

Intel also took some time to publish a press release on the Xeon W-3500 and W-2500 workstation CPUs. Intel states that these chips will help people in the workplace to get more AI tasks done, and faster.

The Xeon W-3500 will feature 60 Performance-cores and 120 threads, which adds up to eight more cores over the previous W-3400 chips. Meanwhile, the Xeon W-2500 processor stack uses 26 Performance-cores and 52 threads, two cores above its predecessor, the W-2400.

Intel is rolling out these two chips starting today, and we'll see systems using these chips as early as September. In terms of price, Intel is weirdly coy over how much they'll go for - all it claims is that the range of new Xeon W chips "starts at $609 (Xeon w3-2525) and scales to $5,889 (Xeon w9-3595X)," which is a pretty huge margin for the W-3500 and the W-2500 to fall into.

Finally, Intel took the time to list out everything these two processors can achieve:

  • Up to 4.8 gigahertz (GHz) turbo frequency on W-3500 and W-2500 workstation processors.
  • Up to 112.5 megabytes (MB) (w9-3595X) and 48.75MB (w7-2595X) of L3 cache for better performance and data management – up from 105MB and 45MB in the prior generation, respectively.
  • Eight channels of DDR5 RDIMM (up to 4,800 megatransfers per second) memory support enables up to 4 terabytes (TB) of memory capacity for large datasets and memory-intensive workloads.
  • Continued support for ECC memory and RAS technologies that improve the integrity of critical data and system reliability.
  • Up to 112 CPU PCIe Gen 5.0 lanes on Xeon W-3500 processors and up to 64 CPU PCIe Gen 5.0 on Xeon W-2500 processors for multi-GPUs, SSDs and network cards.
  • 3rd Generation Intel® Deep Learning Boost (AMX, Bfloat16) and AVX-512 support powers improved artificial intelligence training and inferencing – up to 26% faster performance in crucial data science and AI development workloads4.
  • Integrated Intel® Wi-Fi 6E for the latest network connectivity.
  • Ongoing overclocking support for unlocked processors – including DDR5 XMP 3.0 RDIMM memory overclocking features.
  • Intel vPro® Enterprise technologies for hardware-enhanced security features, firmware version control and remote manageability, enabling easy system deployment into the enterprise environment.
read more

item.title: 5 best Evernote alternatives to create an effective digital cabinet
item.itunes:title: 5 best Evernote alternatives to create an effective digital cabinet
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Obsidian offers free robust features and customization to replicate Evernote with additional functionality for note-takers.
  • Notion provides a modern alternative to Evernote with real-time collaboration and a variety of templates for various use cases.
  • OneNote, a classic rival to Evernote, boasts user-friendly organization, integration with Microsoft services, and math and drawing tools.

Evernote, once a darling of digital note-takers, has been through tough times due to growing criticism from the community. The company replaced its native apps with below-average Electron solutions, hiked subscription prices, and crippled the free plan with a number of limitations, the latest of which permits connection to only a single device. If you are fed up with Evernote’s subpar experience and looking to jump ship, you have come to the right place.

In this article, we explore several top Evernote alternatives, highlighting their unique features and benefits to help you find the ideal digital tool for your notes, ideas, and projects.

Related
5 best tools for creating an effective Personal Knowledge Management (PKM) system

Meet your brain’s new best friends

5

5 Obsidian

Obsidian’s popularity has skyrocketed in recent years. The Markdown-based solution can replicate your Evernote setup and even unlock more functionality for your ultimate digital cabinet. Obsidian allows you to create connections between relevant notes and view their relationships through a specialized graph view.

Obsidian also supports Canvas to enable brainstorming thoughts and ideas on an infinite board with ample tools. Customization is where Obsidian truly shines over its rivals. A specific theme menu allows you to modify the app's appearance, while a separate plugin store lets you find relevant add-ons to enhance its functionality.

You can even use Obsidian to create a Personal Knowledge Management (PKM) system. The best part is its pricing model. All the Obsidian features are free to use. To synchronize your notes across different devices, you only need to purchase the Sync add-on. Alternatively, you can avoid this cost by keeping your Obsidian vaults in Google Drive or OneDrive.

Obsidian
Download Obsidian
Related
11 Obsidian tips and tricks for boosting productivity

Tame your thoughts and craft yourself a digital mind palace with Obsidian

1

4 Notion

Notion is a modern Evernote alternative to capture and organize your thoughts and notes. It uses an intuitive block editor, columns, and ‘/’ command to create ideal pages in no time. Notion is more than just a note-taking app, though.

You can use Notion to manage projects, keep a list of your favorite books, create a habit tracker, manage your calendar events, or even plan your college semester like a pro. The possibilities are endless here. Plus, thanks to a rich Notion library, you don’t even need to start from scratch. There is a template for practically any use case.

Notion also scores high on real-time collaboration. You can invite anyone to your page to manage projects and brainstorm ideas. The company has an optional ($10 per month) AI add-on to write a summary or table, ask a question about a specific page, and create a flowchart based on your text prompts.

Unlike Evernote, Notion offers a solid free plan with a 7-day page history, page analytics, and more. The Plus plan starts at $10 per month.

Notion
See at Notion
Related
10 power tricks every Notion user should know

Complete your ideal Notion setup by following these tips

3 OneNote

Speaking of the top Evernote alternatives, how can we leave OneNote out of the list? Evernote vs. OneNote rivalry goes back decades, and Microsoft’s solution can be an ideal pick for a number of reasons.

OneNote is a feature-rich note-taking app that gets the basics right. Unlike Notion, Craft, and others, it isn’t confusing for new users and comes with a standard notes organization (Notebooks > Sections > Pages), plus an easy-to-use toolbar to help you capture ideas effectively.

OneNote easily beats its rivals with a couple of unique add-ons. First, it has close integration with other Microsoft services like Outlook, Sticky Notes, OneDrive, and Loop. Second, it supports math calculations and drawing tools so that you'll be able to jot down your class notes like a pro.

OneNote offers native applications across all platforms and is free to download and use. You only need to pay for OneDrive storage after you utilize the 5GB of free space.

Microsoft OneNote
See at Microsoft
Related
5 reasons I keep coming back to OneNote

Its wide coverage and efficiencies are second to none

2 Joplin

Like Evernote, if you prefer to manage your notes and tasks from a single interface, look no further and get Joplin. It’s a versatile, free, and open-source solution with a user-friendly interface, end-to-end encryption, and other goodies to replace your dated Evernote setup.

The list of features includes Markdown support, an offline-first approach for privacy-minded folks, reminders with a to-do list, a web clipper, and more. You also have the flexibility to use the cloud storage of your choice to save and sync data. Joplin also offers a range of import options to import your Evernote (ENEX) files with a single click.

Although Joplin offers a paid plan at $5.20 per month, most users should be completely fine with its free offering. The paid version unlocks niche features like real-time collaboration and customized note publishing on the web.

Joplin
See at Joplin
Related
OneNote vs. Joplin: Which note-taking app is better?

The ultimate face-off between OneNote and Joplin to see which app reigns supreme

4

1 UpNote

UpNote is easily the top alternative to Evernote. Unlike other note-taking apps, it doesn’t feel bloated with unnecessary AI add-ons, block editor, web publishing, or others. UpNote is laser-focused on taking notes and does the job without breaking a sweat.

In many ways, it’s quite similar to Evernote. You can create multiple notebooks with eye-catching banners, pin your favorite ones for easy access, use tags to organize your notes smartly, and password-protect personal notes from prying eyes.

UpNote lets you connect related notes, offers a sticky window to take notes during online conferences, a focus mode to eliminate distractions, and a web clipper for popular desktop browsers. You can create different spaces to keep your personal notes away from work ones. There is also support for templates and the ability to share your notes on the web.

UpNote has native apps on all the platforms, including Linux. The company's pricing is impressive, at just $2 per month or a $40 lifetime plan. It seems like a steal compared to Evernote and other costly productivity tools.

UpNote
See at UpNote

Create a seamless organization

While there is no shortage of Evernote alternatives, you can’t go wrong with any of our shortlisted picks here. Obsidian and OneNote provide robust, free functionality, while Joplin offers an open-source alternative. Notion caters to various needs for building an optimal personal knowledge management system, and UpNote excels in delivering a reliable and enjoyable note-taking experience.

Check out our separate comparison guide if you want to learn more about the differences between OneNote and Notion.

Speaking of the top Evernote alternatives, how can we leave OneNote out of the list? Evernote vs. OneNote rivalry goes back decades, and Microsoft’s solution can be an ideal pick for a number of reasons.

OneNote is a feature-rich note-taking app that gets the basics right. Unlike Notion, Craft, and others, it isn’t confusing for new users and comes with a standard notes organization (Notebooks > Sections > Pages), plus an easy-to-use toolbar to help you capture ideas effectively.

OneNote easily beats its rivals with a couple of unique add-ons. First, it has close integration with other Microsoft services like Outlook, Sticky Notes, OneDrive, and Loop. Second, it supports math calculations and drawing tools so that you'll be able to jot down your class notes like a pro.

OneNote offers native applications across all platforms and is free to download and use. You only need to pay for OneDrive storage after you utilize the 5GB of free space.

Microsoft OneNote
See at Microsoft
Related
5 reasons I keep coming back to OneNote

Its wide coverage and efficiencies are second to none

2 Joplin

Like Evernote, if you prefer to manage your notes and tasks from a single interface, look no further and get Joplin. It’s a versatile, free, and open-source solution with a user-friendly interface, end-to-end encryption, and other goodies to replace your dated Evernote setup.

The list of features includes Markdown support, an offline-first approach for privacy-minded folks, reminders with a to-do list, a web clipper, and more. You also have the flexibility to use the cloud storage of your choice to save and sync data. Joplin also offers a range of import options to import your Evernote (ENEX) files with a single click.

Although Joplin offers a paid plan at $5.20 per month, most users should be completely fine with its free offering. The paid version unlocks niche features like real-time collaboration and customized note publishing on the web.

Joplin
See at Joplin
Related
OneNote vs. Joplin: Which note-taking app is better?

The ultimate face-off between OneNote and Joplin to see which app reigns supreme

4

1 UpNote

UpNote is easily the top alternative to Evernote. Unlike other note-taking apps, it doesn’t feel bloated with unnecessary AI add-ons, block editor, web publishing, or others. UpNote is laser-focused on taking notes and does the job without breaking a sweat.

In many ways, it’s quite similar to Evernote. You can create multiple notebooks with eye-catching banners, pin your favorite ones for easy access, use tags to organize your notes smartly, and password-protect personal notes from prying eyes.

UpNote lets you connect related notes, offers a sticky window to take notes during online conferences, a focus mode to eliminate distractions, and a web clipper for popular desktop browsers. You can create different spaces to keep your personal notes away from work ones. There is also support for templates and the ability to share your notes on the web.

UpNote has native apps on all the platforms, including Linux. The company's pricing is impressive, at just $2 per month or a $40 lifetime plan. It seems like a steal compared to Evernote and other costly productivity tools.

UpNote
See at UpNote

Create a seamless organization

While there is no shortage of Evernote alternatives, you can’t go wrong with any of our shortlisted picks here. Obsidian and OneNote provide robust, free functionality, while Joplin offers an open-source alternative. Notion caters to various needs for building an optimal personal knowledge management system, and UpNote excels in delivering a reliable and enjoyable note-taking experience.

Check out our separate comparison guide if you want to learn more about the differences between OneNote and Notion.

item.title: 5 alternatives to Notepad++ on Mac
item.itunes:title: 5 alternatives to Notepad++ on Mac
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Brackets, CodeRunner 4, and Nova 3 are top alternatives to Notepad++ on Mac, offering unique features for web design and development.
  • Each alternative has its own set of tools and extensions, ranging from live preview to code completion, to suit different programming needs.
  • Visual Studio Code and Sublime Text are also popular Notepad++ alternatives on Mac, providing a customizable and efficient text editing experience.

Thanks to remarkable execution speed, syntax highlighting, multi-view, macro reading, and customization options, Notepad++ is easily one of the top text and source code editors out there. However, it’s only available on the Windows platform. Thankfully, there is no shortage of Notepad++ alternatives for macOS users. You can pick one based on your preferences, budget, and features on offer. Continue reading to learn about the top Notepad++ equivalents for Mac.

Related
Best productivity apps for Mac in 2024

There are so many great third-party and Apple-branded productivity apps for Mac, so we will highlight 10 of the best

6 Brackets

Source: Brackets

Maintained by Adobe and aimed at web designers and front-end developers, Brackets is free, open-source, and one of the top alternatives to Notepad++ on Mac. Developers primarily use it for web design and development, and the software is written in HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.

One of the really useful features of Brackets is Live Preview. As the name suggests, when you tweak CSS and HTML, you'll see changes live on your browser tab. Simply use the cmd + option + P keys to enable live preview. Aside from live preview, it supports inline editors, preprocessor support, color, gradient, and image preview.

You can also head to the Extension Manager in Brackets and download some of the popular add-ons like Auto Close Tag, Color Highlighter, Autoprefixer, Beautify, Emmet, and more (make sure to check extension compatibility with your Brackets version).

Brackets
See at Brackets

5 CodeRunner 4

Source: CodeRunner

CodeRunner 4 is a solid, lightweight IDE (Integrated Development Environment) programming editor for Mac. The latest CodeRunner 4 has native support for Apple Silicon and comes with a bunch of useful features to complete your project like a pro. It supports 25 languages, including the likes of AppleScript, C, C++, JavaScript, HTML, jQuery, Node.js and more. That said, the code completion and debugger support do vary from one language to another. Make sure to check the company’s language overview page before hitting the buy button.

Other features include a code tokenizer for Swift, a sidebar search option, improvements to printing, macOS compatibility, debugging, and more. It supports dark mode, too. CodeRunner 4 is priced at $20 and it’s available from the Mac App Store.

CodeRunner
See at Apple App Store

4 Nova 3

Nova 3 is another feature-rich native Notepad++ alternative on Mac. The built-in text editor is the heart and soul of Nova 3. It’s a flexible text editor with useful functions like autocomplete, tag pairs, brackets, a Minimap, and more.

Nova also has built-in debugging support for popular programming languages like PHP, Python, Node.js, Deno, and Chrome. It is huge time-saver for developers. You can also dig Nova’s extensions library to add support for more languages or create your own using the company’s extensions API.

Some of the popular extensions are Beautify, Ashokai, Rust, Python, TypeScript, Deno, Copilot for Nova, and more. The built-in tools like a local file browser, text clips, Git, task reports, file tracking, and publishing improve your workflow and boost productivity. You can also split the sidebar to check multiple tools simultaneously. Nova costs $99 as a one-time purchase.

Nova
See at Nova

3 BBEdit

Source: BBEdit

BBEdit is another robust text editing with a long list of text editing capabilities. It offers a clean and efficient interface, powerful search & replace, built-in syntax highlighting, code folding for numerous programming languages, and all the necessary development tools like HTML and CSS markup tools, live preview, and built-in validation.

While it was already a capable Notepad++ alternative on Mac, the company took it to the next level with the BBEdit version 15. It finally introduces a bunch of much-awaited features, such as a minimap to glance over your active text document, an expandable cheat sheet, AI chat worksheets, improvements to the project workspace, and other UI refreshes.

BBEdit 15 costs $40 as a one-time purchase. Existing BBEdit 14 users can purchase it for $30.

BBEdit
See at BBEdit

2 Visual Studio Code

Source: Visual Studio Code

Speaking of the top Novapad++ alternatives on Mac, how can we leave Microsoft’s Visual Studio Code out of the list? Thanks to its long list of features and extensibility, Visual Studio Code is quite popular among developers.

Its IntelliSense is a useful add-on to suggest smart completions based on variable types and function definitions. Like other tools, Visual Studio Code also has a robust extension gallery to streamline your workflow in no time. Some of the popular titles include GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT, Tabnine, Bookmarks, CodeSnap, GitHistory, and more.

VS Code is quite customizable, too. You can play with different themes, keyboard shortcuts, and other preferences. It supports debugging for Node.js, JavaScript, and Typescript by default. Visual Studio Code is free to download and use. The software is available on Windows, Mac, and Linux.

Visual Studio Code
See at Visual Studio Code

1 Sublime Text

Source: Sublime Text

Sublime Text is a capable programming text editor on Mac. Many developers prefer Sublime Text due to its minimalistic UI, useful features, and solid performance. The company also revamped the UI with dark theme support.

The Goto Anything feature can be a huge timesaver for developers. You can simply press the command + P keys and jump to symbols, lines, or words in seconds. You can also multi-select and change several lines simultaneously. Other features include plugin support, quick project switch, split editing, auto-complete, and more.

Sublime Text is a cross-platform solution with native apps on Windows, Linux, and Mac. It’s free to download and use. You do receive frequent pop-ups to buy the software for $99.

Sublime Text
See at Sublime Text

Continue your projects on Mac

The unavailability of Notepad++ on Mac shouldn’t discourage you from exploring Apple’s desktop operating system. Thanks to some of the robust text editors on Mac, we are sure you won’t miss Notepad++ on your Mac. If you are new to macOS, check out the first things to do on your Mac. You can also glance over our separate post to find the top Mac apps.

item.title: As is tradition, AI researchers got Doom running on Stable Diffusion
item.itunes:title: As is tradition, AI researchers got Doom running on Stable Diffusion
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Google researchers got Doom running on an AI image generator.
  • GameNGen project used AI agents to play Doom and simulate gameplay for the image generator.
  • The AI version shows some oddities, but it's an impressive feat overall.

If it has a screen, it can run Doom. This manta has encouraged people to run Doom on all manners of devices, from Def Con badges to lawnmowers. Now, the trend continues, as researchers from Google have gotten a playable version of the classic FPS running within an AI image generator.

Researchers get Doom running on Stable Diffusion

As spotted by Tom's Hardware, this project was developed by several researchers under the name GameNGen. The researchers created AI agents to play many games of Doom, rewarding them when they got pickups and killed enemies and punishing them if they got hurt or died. The researchers could then feed lots of gameplay into a Stable Diffusion model, which "memorized" what the levels looked like, where the enemies spawned, and how the game works.

Once the training had been completed, people could play Doom via Stable Diffusion. The player could give the model inputs, and the AI image generation model would remember what that action meant in terms of the game and replicate them as AI images. This includes fighting demons, with Stable Diffusion simulating both taking out monsters and taking damage from them.

The researchers claim that "human raters are only slightly better than random chance at distinguishing short clips of the game from clips of the simulation," but eagle-eyed Doom fans will see some weirdness with the AI version of the game. For example, the AI has a tough time representing the ammo count at the bottom right, with the numbers morphing into one another regularly. Also, Doom Guy's portrait either jitters uncontrollably or locks itself into one face for long periods. However, it's still an impressive feat to get a playable version of Doom running on an AI image generation mode.

If you'd like to make some AI images yourself, be sure to check out the best AI image generators out there.

item.title: Best motherboards for Intel 14th Gen processors in 2024
item.itunes:title: Best motherboards for Intel 14th Gen processors in 2024
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The 14th Gen Core desktop processor range from Intel codenamed Raptor Lake Refresh, made a splash in 2023 but they were too similar to existing Raptor Lake CPUs to more effectively take on AMD. A positive of this fact is full support on older motherboards, allowing 14th-gen processors to be used on the same platforms as 12th and 13th-gen processors. This updated lineup includes the likes of Intel Core i9-14900KS, which is Intel's first mass-produced processor rated to hit 6.2 GHz. The new Raptor Lake Refresh lineup has some powerful processors and you'll need an equally capable motherboard There's no shortage of motherboards on the market, but we've rounded up some of the best Intel motherboards you can pair with your 14th-generation CPU.

  • Editor's choice
    ASUS ROG Maximus Z790 Hero

    The ASUS ROG Maximus Z790 Hero is our pick for the best overall Intel motherboard, and for good reason. This is one of the best-looking and feature-packed motherboards out there that delivers excellent performance with its 20+1-phase power delivery design, and offers a good set of features too. It's a bit on the expensive side, but you also get a lot for your money, including support for PCIe 5, DDR5 memory kits, and plenty of Thunderbolt ports. It also comes with plenty of RGB lighting and stylish ROG branding to make your PC stand out.

    $525 at Amazon $505 at Newegg $525 at Best Buy
  • Best value
    GIGABYTE Z790 AORUS Elite AX

    The Gigabyte Z790 AORUS Elite AX is a great midrange motherboard that's built around Intel's Z790 chipset. This ATX motherboard has plenty of things going for it including support for DDR5-7600 RAM kits, plenty of M.2 sockets and SATA ports for storage, a highly capable 19-phase power setup, and more. It also has a robust feature set with multiple aRGB headers, USB ports, Wi-Fi 6E, 2.5GbE LAN port, and more. This motherboard also looks great with its multi-layered matte black PCB, and brushed aluminum finish on the VRM heatsink.

    $200 at Amazon $230 at Newegg
  • Best performance
    ASUS ROG Maximus Z790 Dark Hero

    If all you seek is a rock-solid foundation for a 14th-gen Intel PC build, look no further than the Asus ROG Maximus Z790 Dark Hero. This is one serious motherboard without going overboard on features. It's a little on the pricey end of the spectrum, but you get a lot for your money. You won't have any trouble powering even the 14900KS with this thing.

    $700 at Amazon $700 at Newegg
  • Best for enthusiasts
    ASRock Z790 Taichi Lite

    ASRock's Z790 Taichi Lite is a cheaper version of the Z790 Taichi but with practically all the same hardware features, minus some premium but ultimately superficial components. This particular motherboard is good enough to power even the most demanding Intel CPUs out there like the Core i9-14900K at full bore. It supports PCIe 5 and DDR5-7600 kits, and it also brings plenty of ports, Wi-Fi 6E, Bluetooth 5.3, 2.5G LAN, and more. It may not the best-looking motherboard on this list, but is definitely a top-performer.

    $342 at Newegg
  • Best white motherboard
    Gigabyte Z790 AORUS Pro X

    The Gigabyte Z790 AORUS Pro X boasts a distinctive all-white design that exudes uniqueness. Its well-diffused RGB lighting complements the aesthetic perfectly. It even excels in overclocking, thanks to its strong 18+1+2 VRM design with 90A SPS power stages. Exceptional connectivity options include five M.2 slots, a 5GbE LAN port, W-iFi 7, and numerous high-speed USB ports. With a durable 8-layer PCB and a reinforced PCIe slot, it manages to merge style with reliability.

    $300 at Amazon $300 at Newegg
  • Best DDR4 motherboard
    ASUS TUF Gaming Z790-Plus WiFi D4

    The Asus TUF Z790-PLUS D4 is a compelling DDR4 motherboard for those seeking value or compatibility with existing DDR4 memory kits. While its design is unremarkable, with minimal RGB lighting, it offers exceptional build quality with a 6-layer PCB, TUF chokes, and TUF 5K black metallic capacitors. The feature set is basic for the price, featuring 2.5GbE LAN, Wi-Fi 6, four M.2 slots, and a noteworthy USB 3.2 Gen2x2 Type-C port, making it an affordable yet capable choice.

    $200 at Amazon $200 at Newegg
  • Best Mini-ITX motherboard
    ASUS ROG Strix B760-I Gaming WIFI

    The ASUS ROG Strix Z790-I is the ideal Mini ITX motherboard for compact gaming PCs, offering an impressive 8-layer PCB, efficient M.2 heatsinks, and a reinforced PCIe 5.0 slot. This board is not built for overclocking, but its stock performance is admirable, thanks to the 8+1 phase VRM design with 80A power stages. The board also delivers a respectable feature set, highlighted by its two M.2 slots and rear USB 3.2 Gen 2×2 Type-C port.

    $200 at Amazon
  • Best budget
    MSI PRO Z790-S WIFI

    The MSI PRO Z790-S motherboard doesn't come with a lot of bells and whistles, but it delivers all the essentials and lets you build a solid PC with Intel's 14th gen processors without any issues. One of the best things about this motherboard is that it gets full PCIe 5 and DDR5 support at this price, which is quite impressive. The power delivery system on this motherboard is also capable enough to run CPUs like Intel Core i7-14700K at stock settings, which is plenty for an ITX build.

    $159 at Amazon $159 at Newegg

Which Intel 14th-Gen motherboard should you buy?

With a plethora of great options available on the market, you might be wondering which motherboard you should choose for your new Intel build. Those who don't want to go over all the picks can consider either the ASUS ROG Maximus Z790 or the Gigabyte Z790 Aorus Elite AX, both of which offer the flagship Z790 chipset while being quite reasonably priced. They also offer an excellent power delivery system and a host of modern connectivity features, fit for a reliable built with Intel's 14th gen CPUs.

Those who are on a tight budget, however, should consider the MSI PRO Z790-S WiFi motherboard. It may not have all the bells and whistles, but it offers great value for your money by offering the flagship-grade Z790 chipset and features like DDR5 and PCIe 5 support in this price range. We've also highlighted some other options, including the ASUS ROG Strix B760-I and the Gigabyte Z790 AORUS Pro X for those who fancy a mini-ITX or a white-themed PC, respectively.
item.description:
The 14th Gen Core desktop processor range from Intel codenamed Raptor Lake Refresh, made a splash in 2023 but they were too similar to existing Raptor Lake CPUs to more effectively take on AMD. A positive of this fact is full support on older motherboards, allowing 14th-gen processors to be used on the same platforms as 12th and 13th-gen processors. This updated lineup includes the likes of Intel Core i9-14900KS, which is Intel's first mass-produced processor rated to hit 6.2 GHz. The new Raptor Lake Refresh lineup has some powerful processors and you'll need an equally capable motherboard There's no shortage of motherboards on the market, but we've rounded up some of the best Intel motherboards you can pair with your 14th-generation CPU.

  • Editor's choice
    ASUS ROG Maximus Z790 Hero

    The ASUS ROG Maximus Z790 Hero is our pick for the best overall Intel motherboard, and for good reason. This is one of the best-looking and feature-packed motherboards out there that delivers excellent performance with its 20+1-phase power delivery design, and offers a good set of features too. It's a bit on the expensive side, but you also get a lot for your money, including support for PCIe 5, DDR5 memory kits, and plenty of Thunderbolt ports. It also comes with plenty of RGB lighting and stylish ROG branding to make your PC stand out.

    $525 at Amazon $505 at Newegg $525 at Best Buy
  • Best value
    GIGABYTE Z790 AORUS Elite AX

    The Gigabyte Z790 AORUS Elite AX is a great midrange motherboard that's built around Intel's Z790 chipset. This ATX motherboard has plenty of things going for it including support for DDR5-7600 RAM kits, plenty of M.2 sockets and SATA ports for storage, a highly capable 19-phase power setup, and more. It also has a robust feature set with multiple aRGB headers, USB ports, Wi-Fi 6E, 2.5GbE LAN port, and more. This motherboard also looks great with its multi-layered matte black PCB, and brushed aluminum finish on the VRM heatsink.

    $200 at Amazon $230 at Newegg
  • Best performance
    ASUS ROG Maximus Z790 Dark Hero

    If all you seek is a rock-solid foundation for a 14th-gen Intel PC build, look no further than the Asus ROG Maximus Z790 Dark Hero. This is one serious motherboard without going overboard on features. It's a little on the pricey end of the spectrum, but you get a lot for your money. You won't have any trouble powering even the 14900KS with this thing.

    $700 at Amazon $700 at Newegg
  • Best for enthusiasts
    ASRock Z790 Taichi Lite

    ASRock's Z790 Taichi Lite is a cheaper version of the Z790 Taichi but with practically all the same hardware features, minus some premium but ultimately superficial components. This particular motherboard is good enough to power even the most demanding Intel CPUs out there like the Core i9-14900K at full bore. It supports PCIe 5 and DDR5-7600 kits, and it also brings plenty of ports, Wi-Fi 6E, Bluetooth 5.3, 2.5G LAN, and more. It may not the best-looking motherboard on this list, but is definitely a top-performer.

    $342 at Newegg
  • Best white motherboard

It even excels in overclocking, thanks to its strong 18+1+2 VRM design with 90A SPS power stages. Exceptional connectivity options include five M.2 slots, a 5GbE LAN port, W-iFi 7, and numerous high-speed USB ports. With a durable 8-layer PCB and a reinforced PCIe slot, it manages to merge style with reliability.

    $300 at Amazon $300 at Newegg
  • Best DDR4 motherboard
    ASUS TUF Gaming Z790-Plus WiFi D4

    The Asus TUF Z790-PLUS D4 is a compelling DDR4 motherboard for those seeking value or compatibility with existing DDR4 memory kits. While its design is unremarkable, with minimal RGB lighting, it offers exceptional build quality with a 6-layer PCB, TUF chokes, and TUF 5K black metallic capacitors. The feature set is basic for the price, featuring 2.5GbE LAN, Wi-Fi 6, four M.2 slots, and a noteworthy USB 3.2 Gen2x2 Type-C port, making it an affordable yet capable choice.

    $200 at Amazon $200 at Newegg
  • Best Mini-ITX motherboard
    ASUS ROG Strix B760-I Gaming WIFI

    The ASUS ROG Strix Z790-I is the ideal Mini ITX motherboard for compact gaming PCs, offering an impressive 8-layer PCB, efficient M.2 heatsinks, and a reinforced PCIe 5.0 slot. This board is not built for overclocking, but its stock performance is admirable, thanks to the 8+1 phase VRM design with 80A power stages. The board also delivers a respectable feature set, highlighted by its two M.2 slots and rear USB 3.2 Gen 2×2 Type-C port.

    $200 at Amazon
  • Best budget
    MSI PRO Z790-S WIFI

    The MSI PRO Z790-S motherboard doesn't come with a lot of bells and whistles, but it delivers all the essentials and lets you build a solid PC with Intel's 14th gen processors without any issues. One of the best things about this motherboard is that it gets full PCIe 5 and DDR5 support at this price, which is quite impressive. The power delivery system on this motherboard is also capable enough to run CPUs like Intel Core i7-14700K at stock settings, which is plenty for an ITX build.

    $159 at Amazon $159 at Newegg

Which Intel 14th-Gen motherboard should you buy?

With a plethora of great options available on the market, you might be wondering which motherboard you should choose for your new Intel build. Those who don't want to go over all the picks can consider either the ASUS ROG Maximus Z790 or the Gigabyte Z790 Aorus Elite AX, both of which offer the flagship Z790 chipset while being quite reasonably priced. They also offer an excellent power delivery system and a host of modern connectivity features, fit for a reliable built with Intel's 14th gen CPUs.

Those who are on a tight budget, however, should consider the MSI PRO Z790-S WiFi motherboard. It may not have all the bells and whistles, but it offers great value for your money by offering the flagship-grade Z790 chipset and features like DDR5 and PCIe 5 support in this price range. We've also highlighted some other options, including the ASUS ROG Strix B760-I and the Gigabyte Z790 AORUS Pro X for those who fancy a mini-ITX or a white-themed PC, respectively.
